title: BATTLE OF THE ROCKETS 2026
what: Designed, analyzed, and launched two high-power rockets for the 2026 Battle of the Rockets competition: a Mars Rover delivery vehicle (target apogee 1,000–2,000 ft) and a Target Altitude rocket (801 ft, tube fins only)
Led aerodynamic design through multi-physics simulation, fin configuration optimization, and full vehicle CAD integration

Contributed CFD analysis (unsteady URANS in OpenFOAM) to quantify instantaneous drag under accelerating subsonic flow — decomposing pressure vs. viscous contributions and validating design choices under non-equilibrium conditions
how: Aerodynamic design: iterated trapezoidal and tube fin geometries in SolidWorks; ran RockSim stability sweeps to balance drag, stability margin, and manufacturability
CFD validation: built custom OpenFOAM URANS pipeline (snappyHexMesh, k-ω SST turbulence, time-dependent inlet BC); post-processed drag evolution in ParaView to inform nose cone optimization

Fabrication: epoxied tube fins to phenolic airframe, managed budget/BOM tracking, coordinated with recovery and payload subteams; transitioned from Blue Tube to phenolic after warping failure and redesigned aft section from 3-fin to 6-fin mid-project to recover static margin
results: Mars Rover: 1,459 ft apogee (within 1,000–2,000 ft spec) — successful rover deployment and recovery, <20 ft/s descent
Altitude Rocket: 758 ft apogee — 43 ft from 801 ft target (<6% error), fully recovered
CFD identified nose cone pressure drag as dominant contributor for the Altitude vehicle; validated smooth flow attachment (no separation) and quadratic drag scaling under acceleration
Coordinated CAD, simulation, and physical build across 5-person team; debugged motor igniter slippage at competition; methodology contributed to BNL-level research publication
